philosophicalDetails.definition: Divine powers, decrees, fundamental attributes of civilization

connections: Abzu - fresh water abyss beneath earth
name: Me (Divine Powers)
description: Also known as ✨ Me - Divine Powers.

richMetadata.structure: The Anunnaki are organized hierarchically under Anu (sky god). They inhabit multiple levels: high Anunnaki (council in heaven), middle Anunnaki (executive functions), and lower Anunnaki (workers). They gather in Nippur and convene in divine assembly (Ekur) for cosmic decisions.
connections: Abzu - fresh water abyss beneath earth
richMetadata.significance: The Anunnaki represent divine bureaucracy and cosmic administration. They created humans to serve them and maintain cosmic order. Their existence illustrates Sumerian understanding of divine hierarchy, duty, and the interplay between celestial and terrestrial realms. The Anunnaki embody the principle of divine work and governance.
sources: Descent of Inanna - journey through realms
Cuneiform texts from Nippur and Sumeria

keyMyths.title: Inanna's Descent to the Underworld
keyMyths.description: Inanna, Queen of Heaven, descends to the underworld, ruled by her sister Ereshkigal, to expand her dominion. She must relinquish her me one by one at each gate, symbolizing the relinquishment of power, ultimately facing death and resurrection through the intervention of Enki.
keyMyths.source: Inanna's Descent to the Underworld (ETCSL t.1.4.1)

keyMyths.title: Inanna and Enki: The Transfer of the Arts of Civilization from Eridu to Uruk
keyMyths.description: Inanna cunningly obtains the me from Enki, the god of wisdom in Eridu, by intoxicating him. She then transports them to her city of Uruk, establishing its cultural and political supremacy and bringing civilization to its people.

keyMyths.title: The Creation Myth
keyMyths.description: The creation myth describes the separation of heaven and earth, and the creation of humankind. The me are essential to the proper functioning of the cosmos and the roles of deities and mortals within it, establishing order from chaos.

keyMyths.title: The Myth of Adapa
keyMyths.description: Adapa, a wise human created by Enki, is summoned to heaven and offered immortality. He refuses the food of life, influenced by Enki's deceptive advice, thereby condemning humanity to mortality, highlighting the limitations imposed by the me.

keyMyths.title: The Instructions of Shuruppak
keyMyths.description: This wisdom text, attributed to Shuruppak, provides moral and practical advice for living a just and prosperous life. It implicitly acknowledges the existence of the me by outlining the proper societal order and behavior, reflecting the established divine norms.

sources.name: Electronic Text Corpus of Sumerian Literature (ETCSL)
sources.description: A comprehensive online resource providing translations and transliterations of Sumerian literary texts.
sources.title: Electronic Text Corpus of Sumerian Literature (ETCSL)

sources.name: Black, J., Cunningham, G., Flückiger-Hawker, E., Robson, E., & Zólyomi, G. (2006). *The Literature of Ancient Sumer*. Oxford University Press.
sources.description: A standard academic compilation of Sumerian literature with detailed commentary.
sources.title: Black, J., Cunningham, G., Flückiger-Hawker, E., Robson, E., & Zólyomi, G. (2006). *The Literature of Ancient Sumer*. Oxford University Press.

sources.name: Bottéro, J. (2001). *Religion in Ancient Mesopotamia*. University of Chicago Press.
sources.description: An analysis of Mesopotamian religious beliefs and practices.
sources.title: Bottéro, J. (2001). *Religion in Ancient Mesopotamia*. University of Chicago Press.

sources.name: Enuma Elish
sources.description: The Babylonian creation epic, providing insight into Mesopotamian cosmology.
sources.title: Enuma Elish

sources.name: Hallo, W. W., & Younger, K. L. (Eds.). (2000). *The Context of Scripture: Canonical Compositions, Monumental Inscriptions, and Archival Documents from the Biblical World*. Brill.
sources.description: A collection of translated ancient Near Eastern texts, providing context for understanding the Bible and Mesopotamian culture.
sources.title: Hallo, W. W., & Younger, K. L. (Eds.). (2000). *The Context of Scripture: Canonical Compositions, Monumental Inscriptions, and Archival Documents from the Biblical World*. Brill.
